definition: An open, cache-coherent interconnect built on the PCIe physical layer that lets CPUs, accelerators, and memory share data coherently — enabling memory expansion, pooling, and disaggregation.
CXL's three sub-protocols: CXL.io (PCIe-like discovery/IO), CXL.cache (device caches host memory coherently), CXL.mem (host accesses device-attached memory). Devices mix these by type.
Type 1 = accelerators with caches (no local memory); Type 2 = accelerators with their own memory (GPUs, coherent); Type 3 = memory expanders/buffers. Type 3 drives the memory-expansion use case.
Runs on PCIe 6.0 (64 GT/s), adds multi-level switching, fabric topologies, and memory sharing (coherent access to the same memory by multiple hosts) — enabling true composable/disaggregated systems.
Aggregating memory into a shared pool that multiple servers draw from on demand, reducing stranded/over-provisioned DRAM and improving utilization across a rack.
Using CXL-attached memory as a slower-but-larger tier below local DRAM, with the OS/software moving hot data up and cold data down — a practical way to expand capacity affordably.
